# Ambient Discoverability

**Flexibility: Expected (SHOULD)**

> **Vocabulary update (per JOS-D0077, 2026-05-20):** "resource" / "governed object" → **component** everywhere; the registry is the **Component Registry**. See [JOS-C49] for the parts-on-a-shelf framing.

## The Commitment

Every component (capability, pattern, template, tool, governance object,
spec, gate, method — anything JOS knows about) SHOULD be discoverable
without knowing what you're looking for. The inventory inspires the
solution. Browsing the shelf is itself a creative act.

## What This Means

- Every component category has a browsable registry (per [JOS-SPEC-021]).
- New participants can discover what exists within 30 seconds of arriving
  (today: by opening `.jos/COMPONENTS-CHEATSHEET.md` once Phase 3 lands).
- Components surface themselves through the Component Registry, not through
  tribal knowledge.
- The absence of a component from the registry means it doesn't exist
  (no shadow components — per [JOS-R02]).

## Violation Looks Like

A useful template exists but nobody knows about it because it's in a subdirectory
with no registry entry. A capability is available but only discoverable by
asking the right person. Governance rules exist that participants have never seen.

## Derived From

- JOS-C14 (Ambient Discoverability) — the concept behind this principle
- **JOS-C49 (Components as Parts on a Shelf)** — extends C14 with the
  shelf framing + canonical `component` vocabulary
- **JOS-D0077** — Components-as-Parts-on-a-Shelf decision
- **JOS-SPEC-021** — Registry/Index/Manifest schema
- JOS-C01 (Fractal Self-Similarity) — registries are fractal

### Framework Connection

Framework §2 (lens navigation) makes discoverability ambient by allowing users to anchor at any node and browse L0-L3, while §7 (Empty/Null/Filled states) ensures even unfilled slots are visible — you can discover what is possible, not just what exists.
